P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
May 10, 1982 
Mr. L. B. Elam 
Sacramento County Counsel 
700 H Street, Suite 2650 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Re: 
Request for Written Advice on Behalf of 
Supervisor Sandra Smoley, Our No. A-82-054 
Dear Mr. Elam: 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322·6444 
Thank you for your request for written advice on behalf 
of Supervisor Sandra Smoley. 
As we understand them, the 
material facts are as follows: 
FACTS 
(1) 
Sacramento County Supervisor Sandra Smoley (Smoley) 
is an employee of OlDay Travel Agency (OlDay), situated in 
and doing business in Sacramento County. 
(2) 
OIDay is a California, for-profit corporation 
which arranges transportation, lodging and other services 
associated with business and recreational travel. 
With 
respect to domestic travel, OlDay receives a 10% commission 
on the amount of the air fare. 
For foreign travel, the 
commission is 8% of the air fare. 
The commission which 
OlDay receives from lodging and other services is somewhat 
higher than that received from air fare. 
(3) 
Smoley is an employee, not an officer, of OlDay_ 
She does not own stock or have any other proprietary interest 
in OlDay. 
She is paid $500 per month in straight salary 
which compensates Smoley for the services which she performs 
in persuading businesses and other entities to arrange their 
commercial travel through OIDay. 
In addition, she receives 
a commission on these sales. 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Two 
(4) 
Smoley receives a commission upon any contract for 
commercial travel by which a business entity binds itself to 
arrange such travel exclusively through O'Day for a term of 
at least one year. 
These contracts are written, and show 
Smoley as the party who arranged the transaction. 
During 
the first year of such a contract, Smoley receives a commis-
sion of 25% of the commission earned by O'Day on the travel. 
During the second year of the contract, Smoley receives a 
commission of 10% of the commission earned by O'Day. 
Smoley 
receives no commission for travel during any third or later 
year of such a contract. 
Smoley also earns commissions on 
all recreational group tours and recreational cruises arranged 
by her for individuals through O'Day. 
The commission is in 
the amount of 40% of the commission earned by O'Day on such 
individuals. 
In addition, Smoley leads certain of the group 
tours, and the costs are financed through O'Day from charges 
to the individuals participating in the tours. 
Question No.1 
The first series of q~7stions you ask relate to the 
following probable events.-
Assume that Smoley persuades ABC Stores, Inc., a 
local food chain, to arrange its commercial travel 
through O'Day. 
During each of three successive years 
~ It is the Commission's policy not to respond to 
purely hypothetical questions; however, you have indicated 
(infra) that these questions represent real situations which 
will arise. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18320(g) (4) and 18329(a). 
We are advised by you as follows: 
Ms. Smoley either has very recently been employed 
by O'Day, or her employment is eminent. 
The commission 
rate structure identified in the request is actual. 
The actual amount of salary is somewhat hypothetical, 
in that it has not yet been specifically established. 
However, the amount prescribed in the request is a 
reasonable estimate. 
The identities of parties and facts contained in 
the various questions are purely hypothetical. 
However, 
based upon past practice within this County they are 
illustrative of real life contingencies which will 
arise. 
(continued next page) 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Three 
ABC presents an application for the rezoning of property 
in order to authorize a grocery store, and the application 
is subject to determination by the Board of Supervisors. 
In addition, the Board of Supervisors is required by 
law to let contracts for the purchase by the County of 
food for use in its jails and other operations to the 
lowest responsible bidder pursuant to the receipt of 
competitive proposals. 
During each of three successive 
years, ABC presents a bid in response to the solicitation 
for a proposal. 
Would Supervisor Smoley be disqualified on grounds 
of conflict of interest from voting upon the above 
transactions if: 
a. 
ABC has arranged its commercial travel through 
O'Day on an ad hoc basis, without signing an 
exclusive contract, and Smoley has received 
no commissions as a result of such commercial 
travel; or 
b. 
ABC has signed an exclusive three-year contract 
with O'Day and: 
(i) during the first year 
receives a commission from ABC's travel in 
the amount of $2,000 and Smoley receives a 
commission in the amount of $500; 
(ii) during 
the second year O'Day receives a commission 
from ABC's travel in the amount of $150; and 
(iii) during the third year O'Day receives a 
commission from ABC's travel in the amount of 
$2,500 and Smoley receives no commission? 
(footnote 1, continued) 
Supervisor Smoley expects to be confronted with a 
variety of disqualification type issues as a result of 
her employment by O'Day. 
She desires to be able to 
predict in advance of action when she should disqualify 
herself, and when she need not do so. 
Otherwise, important 
business of the Board may be delayed while such decisions 
are pending. 
However, because specific entities are not named and 
additional facts may not be ascertained, our advice must 
remain somewhat general in nature and cannot carry with it 
the type of immunity which normally would attach pursuant to 
Government Code Section 83ll4(b). 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Four 
ANALYSIS 
The b~7ic conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act-
(the "Act") are contained in Section 87100, 
which provides: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
Financial interests are defined in Section 87103, the 
pertinent provisions of which are as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without regard 
to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars 
(8250) or more in value provided to, received by or 
promised to the public official within 12 months prior 
to the time when the decision is made. 
Section 87103. 
Clearly, O'Day is a source of income to Supervisor 
Smoley. 
However, in order to determine if Smoley has a 
potential conflict of interest vis a vis ABC Stores, Inc., 
we must resolve the question of whether or not ABC is also a 
source of income to her as a result of its travel arrangements 
with O'Day. 
As to question "a," we conclude that ABC is not a source 
of income to Supervisor Smoley. 
The customer of a business 
is not a source of income to an employee whose income is 
unrelated in any way to the customer's patronage of the 
employer. 
2 
Government Code Sections 81000-91014; hereafter all 
statutory re 
rences made are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise s 
cified. 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Five 
As to question "b," however, we conclude that ABC is a 
source of income to Supervisor Smoley. 
In the Commission's Carey opinion21 we considered the situa-
tion where a real estate brokerage firm's commissions on sales 
of real property were automatically (contractually) split with 
the sales agent involved. 
There, we held that the commission 
(income) to the brokerage firm was not all attributable to the 
owners of the firm (employer) because the sales agent had a 
contractual right to his or her share. 
We conclude that the "fee" paid to the business entity 
under the circumstances before us herein is only that portion 
of the total commission retained by the firm. 
We think 
that the proper characterization of the transaction in 
question is that there are, in effect, two separate payments 
subsumed in the one negotiable instrument used by the buyer 
to pay the real estate commission, one payment to the firm 
of Cornish and Carey and another to the salesperson who 
brokered the sale. 
Although the firm of Cornish and Carey may be the 
payee designated on the instrument used by the purchaser to 
pay the commission, this is only done to facilitate payment 
and avoid the necessity of having the purchaser write two 
checks. 
* * * 
We note, moreover, that the payment to the salesperson 
is inseparable from the transaction that produces it. 
In 
other words, the payment is made with funds derived from 
the particular sale that creates the salesperson's right to 
the paymen t. 
Carey Opinion!1 at 101-102. 
The Commission excluded real estate commission fees which 
went straight to the sales agent from being considered as income 
to the real estate firm. 
The logical corrollary ~~ this conclusion 
is that those fees are income to the sales agent.-
31 
Opinion requested by Scott T. Carey, 3 FPPC Opinions 
99, No. 76-087, November 3, 1977, (copy enclosed). 
Id. 
51 
We do not here decide whether O'Day is a source of 
commission income to Smoley; O'Day is already a source of 
income to Smoley as a result of her salary. 
The Commission 
has decided that in some situations both the customer and 
the "employer" are sources of income. 
See advice letter to 
Ronald Kaldor, May 16, 1977, No. A-77-244 (copy enclosed). 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Six 
In the situation at hand, however, the income is tech-
nically derived from the airline, which pays the commission 
to Q'Day, to which Smoley has a contractual right. 
Therefore, 
we must determine if the source of income to Supervisor 
Smoley is ABC or the airline. 
A somewhat similar situation 
was discussed in our advice letter to DOuglgJ A. Lewis on 
behalf of Dr. Black, a school board member.-
There, we 
wrote as follows: 
With respect to your third question, it is our 
conclusion that the patient, not the patient's insurance 
carrier, is the source of income to Dr. Black. 
The 
policy underlying disclosure of certain sources of 
income under the Act ... is stated in Section 81002(d). 
That section provides: 
(d) 
Assets and income of public officials 
which may be materially affected by their official 
actions should be disclosed and in appropriate 
circumstances the officials should be disqualified 
from acting in order that conflicts of interest 
may be avoided; 
We believe that the policy envisions the existence 
of potential conflicts of interest in those cases in 
which certain economic relationships may interfere with 
a public official's exercise of objective judgment in 
making governmental decisions. 
In this case, most 
decisions to enter into an economic relationship with 
the doctor would be made by the patient, not the insur-
ance carrier. 
Since the decision to enter into the 
relationship is made by the patient and not the insurance 
company, it is not likely that the payment by the insur-
ance company on behalf of the patient will influence 
the doctor's government decisions which may affect the 
insurance company. 
* * * 
The exception to this general rule would be if the 
insurer, or some other person or business entity sent 
patients to Dr. Black pursuant to some formal agreement 
or plan. 
Under such circumstances, we might reach a 
different conclusion .... 
Lewis Advice letter. 
_6/ 
Advice letter to Douglas A. Lewis, December 27, 
1977, No. A-77-087 (copy enclosed). 
-; 
~, 
Id. at pp. 2-3. 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Seven 
We feel that, here, ABC is much like the patient of Dr. Black. 
The airline or hotel is like the insurance company. 
Although 
technically the commission earned by Supervisor Smoley is paid 
by the airline or hotel involved, it is the customer, ABC, which 
makes the decision "to enter into an economic relationship" with 
O'Day and hence generate the commission for Smoley. 
And in our 
advice letter to Charles Drescher, we considered a similar situa-
tion and found the source of commission income to a one-half 
owner of a travel ag ency81for reporting purposes} to be the 
customers of the agency.-
Therefore, in response to your question "b" we conclude as 
follows: 
(i) 
For a period of 12 months following receipt of $250 or 
more of the $500 commission, Supervisor Smoley would have to 
disqualify herself from participating in any decision Whic§/ 
would have a material financial effect on ABC Stores, Inc.-
(ii) and (iii) 
Once the 12-month period discussed above 
has expired, Supervisor Smoley would no longer have any duty to 
disqualify herself, unless an additional commission of $250 or 
more was earned vis a vis ABC. 
Question No.2 
The second series of questions relates to the following 
probable events. 
XYZ, Inc. is a corporation formed for the 
purpose of acquiring property, constructing and selling homes. 
Mr. Jones owns 60% of the stock, Mr. Smith owns 36% of the stock, 
and Mr. Black owns 4% of the stock. 
XYZ employs a manager to 
whom it pays a salary of $75,000 per year. 
XYZ retains by 
independent contract an architectural firm, an engineering 
firm and a law firm, whom it compensates on an hourly basis 
for services rendered in connection with its land purchases 
and sales, construction design and improvements, and other 
aspects of the business. 
Each of these firms from time to 
time represent XYZ before the Board of Supervisors in its 
applications to subdivide and rezone land for housing projects. 
~/ Advice letter to Charles Drescher, August 18, 1976, 
No. A-76-l05 (copy enclosed). 
~/ See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 for a definition 
of "material financial effect" (copy enclosed). 
As to the 
competitive bidding issue, we would need more facts in order 
to determine if the Board's "decision" is, in fact, only 
ministerial in nature. 
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(d) (1) 
(copy enclosed). 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Eight 
Each of these firms have been retained by XYZ for several 
years. 
During the past year, XYZ paid $100,000 to the engineering 
firm in fees, $50,000 to the architectural firm in fees and 
$25,000 to the law firm in fees. 
Finally, assume that XYZ 
files two or three applications for approval of tentative 
subdivision maps and for rezoning each year, which are heard 
and decided by the Board of Supervisors. 
Would Supervisor Smoley be disqualified on grounds of 
conflict of interests from voting on a subdivision or rezoning 
application by XYZ, Inc. under any of the following circumstances: 
a. 
If either the architectural firm, engineering firm 
or law firm, upon persuasion by Smoley, were to 
arrange its commercial travel through O'Day on an 
ad hoc basis, without an exclusive contract, and 
Smoley were to receive no commission thereon; 
b. 
If either the architectural firm, engineering firm 
or law firm, upon persuasion by Smoley, were to sign 
an exclusive contract with O'Day for commercial 
travel, and Smoley were to receive during the past 
year commissions in the amount of $100, $500 or $750 
as a resul t of such travel; . 
c. 
If either Mr. Jones, Mr. Smith, Mr. Black or the 
Manager, upon persuasion by Smoley, were to take a 
cruise through O'Day, and Smoley were to receive 
during the past year $100, $500 or $750 in commissions 
as a result of the cruise; 
d. 
If a professional employee, stockholder or partner 
of either of the architectural, engineering or law 
firms, upon persuasion by Smoley, were to participate 
in a group tour through O'Day and within the past 
year Smoley were to receive $100, $500 or $750 in 
commissions as a result of participation by that 
person in the group tour; 
e. 
If the answer to any of the above questions is that 
a disqualification from voting is required, would 
the answer be different if Smoley was unaware of 
such detailed facts as the amount of fees earned by 
the engineering, architectural and law firms, the 
extent of holdings in XYZ by Messrs. Jones, Smith 
and Black, or the exact nature and extent of 
relationship between the professional and the firm 
with which he or she is associated? 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May la, 1982 
Page Nine 
ANALYSIS 
Initially, we must state that this is the type of question 
which we cannot answer in any detail. 
So much of our analysis 
depends upon the specific facts of any individual set of 
circumstances, among them the issues raised by question "e." 
Therefore, we will attempt to provide you and Supervisor 
Smoley with some general analysis and guidance so that you 
may address specific instances yourself. 
If she wishes 
further, specific advice in the future, of course, she may 
request it. 
We will address the subparts of your question 
in order. 
a. 
Based upon these facts, there would be no conflict. 
See our analysis on part "a" of Question No.1, supra. 
b. 
Based upon the facts given, we see no conflict of 
interest. 
The firms would be sources of income to Smoley 
(see analysis of part lib" of Question No.1, supra), but 
XYZ, Inc. would not be. 
However, if additional facts were 
present which would indicate that action on a tentative 
subdivision map would cause any of these firms to lose or 
gain business income from XYZ, Inc., then an analysis would 
have to be made to determine whether or not such a loss or 
gain would be sufficient to constitute a material financial 
effect YB7n the firm which was a source of income to Supervisor 
Smoley.--
If such an effect was foreseeable, then disqualifi-
cation would be required. 
Pursuant to our analysis of part 
lib" of Question 1, the required level of commission to Smoley 
would be at least $250 in order to give rise to any potential 
for conflict. 
c. 
Each of the individuals would become a source of 
income to Smoley if the commission were at the $250 level or 
greater. 
Based upon the facts, there would be a conflict 
only if the tentative map decision meets the test for material 
financial I!7ect on the individual who is a source of income 
to Smoley.--
lQ/ Id. 
See specifically Section l8702(b) (3) (C). 
For 
instance, if a contingency fee were involved in obtaining 
the permit approval, that might constitute a material financial 
effect. 
l!/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8702(b) (3) (D). 
If the 
effect upon XYZ, in turn, has a"s nificant effect" on the 
financial affairs of Jones, Smith, Black or the Manager, 
then the conflict would arise. 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Ten 
d. 
Based upon the facts given, we see no conflict 
arising from these circumstances. 
The prospects of such an 
employee being affected by a decision as to his/her employer's 
client are too remote (absent some specific facts to the 
contrary) to be foreseeable. 
e. 
The answers to these questions are that it may make 
a difference if the knowledge is lacking. 
Section 87100 
requires disqualification only from participation in a decision 
"in which [she] knows or has reason to know [she] has a 
financial interest." 
However, the specific dollar amounts 
mentioned in your letter are not, standing alone, crucial to 
our analysis. 
Question No.4 
From time to time, the Board of Supervisors makes decisions 
upon matters which affect the interests of airlines which 
operate at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport. 
Some of these 
decisions relate to the approval of contracts between the 
County and the airlines by which the airlines derive their 
right to operate at the Airport and pay fees in return. 
Other decisions could relate to allowing new airlines to 
operate at the Airport. 
The airlines affected by these 
decisions pay commissions to O'Day, some of which are received 
by Smoley. 
Would receipt of such commissions disqualify 
Smoley on grounds of conflict of interest from voting upon 
such matters? 
ANALYSIS 
As per our analysis of Question No.1, supra, we would 
conclude that, unless there are facts indicating that the 
airlines have steered business her way or referred clients 
to her, then they would not constitute a source of income to 
Smoley on the basis of the commissions paid to her through 
O'Day and no disqualification would be required. 
However, 
if addition of a new airline to the Sacramento airport would 
greatly increase travel prospects then it might directly 
increase her income by $100 or more, in which case we would 
conclude that there is a material financial effect on her 
under 0r2/regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A) 
or (B).--
Consequently, there is a possibility that, at 
least as to some of the decisions which you posit, Smoley 
would be required to disqualify herself. 
~/ As amended March 1, 1982. 
Since, presumably, 
Smoley is hired, at least in part, to promote air travel 
Sacramentans, there may be a nexus between her income and 
the decisions which she may participate in vis a vis Sacramento 
Metropolitan Airport. 
In addition, some of these decisions 
could conceivably have a material financial effect upon 
OIDay, which is a source of income to Smoley. 
See 2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (3)(C). 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Eleven 
Question No.5 
The final series of questions relate to contracts between 
the County and other public agencies. 
If Smoley were to 
persuade a department of State government, or the State as 
an entity, to arrange commercial travel through O'Day, would 
Smoley be disqualified on grounds of conflict of interests 
from voting upon any contract or other transaction between 
the County and either the State or department of State govern-
ment which has arranged the commercial travel with O'Day or 
any other department of State government? 
The County has 
transferred the Sacramento Medical Center to the University 
of California under a contract by which the County pays to 
the University several million dollars per year for patient 
care. 
That transaction has also precipitated other less 
important contractual relationships between the County and 
University. 
Would a disqualification on grounds of conflict 
of interest from voting upon such matters arise by virtue of 
the existence of a travel arrangement with O'Day by the 
University of California, Davis, or the Davis Medical School, 
or University personnel associated with the Sacramento Medical 
Center, which has been initiated by Smoley? 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 82030(b), certain types of income received 
from state or other governmental entities are not considered 
to be income for the purposes of the Political Reform Act. 
However, this exception is limited to salary and reimbursement 
for expenses. 
Commissions earned by Supervisor Smoley are 
not excluded from the Act's definition of income and, hence, 
~hese governme£1?1 entities would constitute sources of 
income to her.--
Given that these governmental entities would be sources 
of income to Smoley, she would have to disqualify herself 
from participating in any decisions which would foreseeably 
have a material financial effect on any governmental entity 
which had become a source of income to her in the form of 
commissions earned which exceeded $250 in the preceeding 12 
months. 
Q/ See our analysis to Question No.1, part "b," supra. 
We would treat only the contracting state agency as a source of 
income, not the entire State of California. 
The analysis re-
garding personnel would be the same as for Question No.2, part 
"c," supra. 
For instance, if Dr. Q was a source of income to 
Smoley and a decision was before the Supervisors regarding can-
cellation of a health services contract with UCD Medical Center 
which would result in the loss of Dr. Q's employment with the 
Medical Center, then a conflict would exist. 


Mr. L. B. El am 
May 10, 1982 
Page Twelve 
The circumstances which you pose give rise to yet another 
potential for conflict of interests. 
Supervisor Smoley may 
not use her official position to influence another governmental 14/ 
entity in its decision as to where to take its travel business.--
CONCLUSION 
Because of the nature of the questions which you have 
posed, our advice has necessarily been somewhat general. 
However, we trust that our analysis has provided you and 
Supervisor Smoley with guidance in analyzing the specific 
factual situations as they arise. 
Should the supervisor 
desire more detailed analysis, with the attendant immunity, 
as to future decisions involving specified entities, please 
do not hesitate to contact our agency. 
Should you have 
further questions regarding this letter, I may be reached at 
916/322-5901. 
REL:km

RE: 
Request for Written Advice -
Commission No. A-82-054 
Received March 6, 1982 
Dear Mr. Leidigh: 
This letter responds to yours dated March 31, 1982. 
The Sacramento County Supervisor to whom the above referenced 
request for legal advice pertains, is Sandra Smoley. 
The travel 
agency identified in the request is the O'Day Travel Agency. 
Ms. Smoley either has very recently been employed by that Agency, 
or her employment is eminent. 
The commission rate structure 
identified in the request is actual. 
The actual amount of salary 
is somewhat hypothetical, in that it has not yet been specifically 
established. 
However, the amount prescribed in the request is a 
reasonable estimate. 
The identities of parties and facts contained in the various 
questions are purely hypothetical. 
However, based upon past 
practice within this County they are illustrative of real life 
contingencies which will arise. 
Supervisor Smoley expects to be confronted with a variety of 
disqualification type issues as a result of her employment by 
O'Day. 
She desires to be able to predict in advance of action when 
she should disqualify herself, and when she need not do so. 
Other-
wise, important business of the Board may be delayed while such 


Robert E. Leidigh 
-2-
April 2, 1982 
decisions are pending. 
I trust that the foregoing satisfactorily responds to your 
inquiry. 
If, for any reason, the information is still insufficient 
to enable you to proceed with an opinion upon the questions posed, 
please contact me immediately. 
LBE:ph 
Very 
yours, 
L. B. ELAM 
County Counsel 


COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO 
THE COUNTY COUNSEL 
L. B. ELAM 
County Counsel

Assistant County Counsel 
THOMAS A. DARLING 
ROBE RT S. WILLETT 
ROBERT L. PLEINES 
RYAN M. POLSTRA 
Supervising Deputy 
County Counsels 
700 H STREET. SUITE 2650 
SACRAMENTO. CALIFORNIA 95814 
PHONE C916) 440-5544 
March 19, 1982 
Legal Division (Section I) 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 
K Street 
Sacramento, California 95814 
RE: 
Request for Conflict of Interest Opinion 
Gentlemen: 
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSELS 
ALFRED J. GUETLING 
CLEMENT J. DOUGHERTY. JR. 
MONTE L. FULLER 
MELVYN W. PRICE 
RICHARD D. MAYER 
WILLIAM H. FOWLER 
JOSEPH M. TAILLEFER 
J. STEVEN BURRIS 
LAWRENCE A. JONES 
ELAINE P. DIPIETRO 
FRANK M. GARCIA 
MANUEL E. LOPES 
LILLY C. FRAW':;¥ 
H. DESMOND MC.,.-rOSI1. ,\,<'\, 
MARGARET L. HftERTY' , 
JOEL K. UHER ~ 
The purpose of this letter is to request a written opinion 
concerning potential conflict of interest issues which may arise 
out of the independent employment of a member of the Sacramento 
County Board of Supervisors. 
The Supervisor is employed by a travel agency who, for purposes 
of this request, is known as Tourister, Inc. 
Tourister is a 
California for-profit corporation, and arranges transportation, 
lodging and other services associated with business and recreational 
travel. 
With respect to travel within the United States, Tourister 
receives a 10% commission on the amount of air fare. 
For foreign 
travel, the commission received is 8% of the air fare. 
The commission 
which Tourister receives from lodging and other services is somewhat 
higher than that received from air fare. 
The Supervisor is an employee of Tourister, and not an officer. 
The Supervisor does not own stock or any other proprietary interest 
in Tourister. 
The Supervisor receives compensation for employment 
In two forms. 
The first form of compensation is straight salary in the amount 
of $500 per month. 
The salary compensates the Supervisor for 
services performed in persuading business and other firms to arrange 


Legal Division 
-2-
March 19, 1982 
commercial travel for those firms through Tourister. 
The second form of compensation is commissions. 
The Supervisor 
receives a commission upon any contract for commercial travel by 
which a business entity binds itself to arrange such travel exclu-
sively through Tourister for a term of at least one year. 
These 
contracts are written, and show the name of the Supervisor as the 
party who arranged the transaction. 
During the first year of such 
a contract, the Supervisor receives a commission of 25% of the 
commission earned by Tourister on the travel. 
During the second 
year of the contract, the Supervisor receives a commission of 10% 
of the commission earned by Tourister. 
The Supervisor receives 
no commission for travel during any third or later year of such a 
contract. 
The Supervisor also earns commissions on all recreational 
group tours and recreational cruises arranged by the Supervisor for 
individuals through Tourister. 
The commission is in the amount of 
40% of the commission earned by Tourister on such individuals. 
In 
addition, the Supervisor leads certain of the group tours, and 
the costs are financed through Tourister from charges to the indivi-
duals participating in the tours. 
The first series of questions relate to the following hypothetical. 
Assume that the Supervisor persuades ABC Stores, Inc., a local food 
chain, to arrange its commercial travel through Tourister. 
During 
each of three successive years ABC presents an application for the 
rezoning of property in order to authorize a grocery store, and the 
application is subject to determination by the Board of Supervisors. 
In addition, the Board of Supervisors is required by law to let 
contracts for the purchase by the County of food for use in its 
jails and other operations to the lowest responsible bidder pursuant 
to the receipt of competitive proposals. 
During each of three 
successive years, ABC presents a bid in response to the solicitation 
for a proposal. 
Would the Supervisor be disqualified on grounds of conflict 
of interest from voting upon the above transactions if: 
a. 
ABC has arranged its commercial travel through 
Tourister on an ad hoc basis, without signing an 
exclusive contract, and the Supervisor has received 
no commissions as a result of such commercial 
travel; or 
b. 
ABC has signed an exclusive three-year contract 
with Tourister, and: 
(i) during the first year 
Tourister receives a commission from ABC's travel 
in the amount of $2,000 and the Supervisor receives 
a commission in the amount of $500i 
(ii) during the 


Legal Division 
-3-
March 19, 1982 
second year 
Tourister receives a commission from 
ABC's travel in the amount of $1,500, and the 
Supervisor receives a commission in the amount of 
$150; and 
(iii) during the third year Tourister 
receives a commission from ABC's travel in the 
amount of $2,500 and the Supervisor receives no 
commission? 
The second series of questions relates to the following hypo-
thetical. 
XYZ, Inc. is a corporation formed for the purpose of 
acquiring property, constructing and selling homes. 
Mr. Jones 
owns 60% of the stock, Mr. Smith owns 36% of the stock, and Mr. 
Black owns 4% of the stock. 
XYZ employs a manager to whom it pays 
a salary of $75,000 per year. 
XYZ retains by independent contract 
an architectural firm, an engineering firm and a law firm, whom 
it compensates on an hourly basis for services rendered in con-
nection with its land purchases and sales, construction design 
and improvements, and other aspects of the business. 
Each of these 
firms from time to time represent XYZ before the Board of Super-
visors in its applications to subdivide and rezone land for 
housing projects. 
Each of these firms have been retained by XYZ 
for several years. 
During the past year, XYZ paid $100,000 to the 
engineering firm in fees, $50,000 to the architectural firm in 
fees and $25,000 to the law firm in fees. 
Finally, assume that 
XYZ files two or three applications for approval of tentative sub-
division maps and for rezoning each year, which are heard and 
decided by the Board of Supervisors. 
Would the Supervisor be disqualified on grounds of conflict 
of interest from voting on a subdivision or rezoning application 
by XYZ, Inc. under any of the following circumstances: 
a. 
If either the architectural firm, engineering firm 
or law firm, upon persuasion by the Supervisor, 
were to arrange its commercial travel through 
Tourister on an ad hoc basis, without an exclusive 
contract, and the Supervisor were to receive no 
commission thereon; 
b. 
If either the architectural firm, engineering firm 
or law firm, upon persuasion by the Supervisor, 
were to sign an exclusive contract with Tourister 
for commercial travel, and the Supervisor were to 
receive during the past year commissions in the 
amount of $100, $500 or $750 as a result of such 
travel; 
c. 
If either Mr. Jones, Mr. Smith, Mr. Black/ 
or the 
Manager, upon persuasion by the Supervisor, were 
to take a cruise through Tourister, and the Super-
visor were to receive during the past year $100, 


Legal Division 
-4-
March 19, 1982 
$500 or $750 in commissions as a result of the cruise; 
d. 
If a professional employee, stockholder or partner 
of either of the architectural, engineering or 
law firms, upon persuasion by the Supervisor, were 
to participate in a group tour through Tourister, 
and within the past year the Supervisor were to 
receive $100, $500 or $750 in commissions as a 
result of participation by that person in the group 
tour; 
e. 
If the answer to any of the above questions is that 
a disqualification from voting is required, would 
the answer be different if the Supervisor was unaware 
of such detailed facts as the amount of fees earned 
by the engineering, architectural and law firms, 
the extent of holdings in XYZ by Messrs. Jones, 
Smith and Black, or the exact nature and extent 
of the relationship between the professional and 
the firm with which he or she is associated? 
From time to time, the Board of Supervisors makes decisions 
upon matters which affect the interests of airlines which operate 
at Sacramento Metropolitan Airport. 
Some of these decisions relate 
to the approval of contracts between the County and the airlines 
by which the airlines derive their right to operate at the Airport 
and pay fees in return. 
Other decisions could relate to allowing 
new airlines to operate at the Airport. 
The airlines affected by 
these decisions pay commissions to Tourister, some of which are 
received by the Supervisor. 
Would receipt of such commissions 
disqualify on grounds of conflict of interest the Supervisor from 
voting upon such matters? 
The final series of questions relate to contracts between the 
County and other public agencies. 
If the Supervisor were to per-
suade a department of State government, or the State as an entity, 
to arrange commercial travel through Tourister, would the Supervisor 
be disqualified on grounds of conflict of interest from voting upon 
any contract or other transaction between the County and either 
the State or department of State government which has arranged the 
commercial travel with Tourister or any other department of State 
government? 
The County has transferred the Sacramento Medical 
Center to the University of California under a contract by which 
the County pays to the University several million dollars per year 
for patient care. 
That transaction has also precipitated other 
less important contractual relationships between the County and 
University. 
Would a disqualification on grounds of conflict of 
interest from voting upon such matters arise by virtue of the 
existence of a travel arrangement with Tourister by the Universi 


Legal Division 
-5-
March 19, 1982 
of California, Davis, or the Davis Medical School, or University 
personnel associated with the Sacramento Medical Center, which 
has been initiated by the Supervisor? 
The Supervisor in behalf of whom these questions are asked 
is in need of an opinion issued as expeditiously as possible. 
Your cooperation will be appreciated. 
LBE:ph 
Very truly yours, 
L. B. ELAM 
County Counsel

sections.question:
No.1 
The first series of q~7stions you ask relate to the 
following probable events.-
Assume that Smoley persuades ABC Stores, Inc., a 
local food chain, to arrange its commercial travel 
through O'Day. 
During each of three successive years 
~ It is the Commission's policy not to respond to 
purely hypothetical questions; however, you have indicated 
(infra) that these questions represent real situations which 
will arise. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18320(g) (4) and 18329(a). 
We are advised by you as follows: 
Ms. Smoley either has very recently been employed 
by O'Day, or her employment is eminent. 
The commission 
rate structure identified in the request is actual. 
The actual amount of salary is somewhat hypothetical, 
in that it has not yet been specifically established. 
However, the amount prescribed in the request is a 
reasonable estimate. 
The identities of parties and facts contained in 
the various questions are purely hypothetical. 
However, 
based upon past practice within this County they are 
illustrative of real life contingencies which will 
arise. 
(continued next page) 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Three 
ABC presents an application for the rezoning of property 
in order to authorize a grocery store, and the application 
is subject to determination by the Board of Supervisors. 
In addition, the Board of Supervisors is required by 
law to let contracts for the purchase by the County of 
food for use in its jails and other operations to the 
lowest responsible bidder pursuant to the receipt of 
competitive proposals. 
During each of three successive 
years, ABC presents a bid in response to the solicitation 
for a proposal. 
Would Supervisor Smoley be disqualified on grounds 
of conflict of interest from voting upon the above 
transactions if: 
a. 
ABC has arranged its commercial travel through 
O'Day on an ad hoc basis, without signing an 
exclusive contract, and Smoley has received 
no commissions as a result of such commercial 
travel; or 
b. 
ABC has signed an exclusive three-year contract 
with O'Day and: 
(i) during the first year 
receives a commission from ABC's travel in 
the amount of $2,000 and Smoley receives a 
commission in the amount of $500; 
(ii) during 
the second year O'Day receives a commission 
from ABC's travel in the amount of $150; and 
(iii) during the third year O'Day receives a 
commission from ABC's travel in the amount of 
$2,500 and Smoley receives no commission? 
(footnote 1, continued) 
Supervisor Smoley expects to be confronted with a 
variety of disqualification type issues as a result of 
her employment by O'Day. 
She desires to be able to 
predict in advance of action when she should disqualify 
herself, and when she need not do so. 
Otherwise, important 
business of the Board may be delayed while such decisions 
are pending. 
However, because specific entities are not named and 
additional facts may not be ascertained, our advice must 
remain somewhat general in nature and cannot carry with it 
the type of immunity which normally would attach pursuant to 
Government Code Section 83ll4(b). 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Four
sections.conclusion:
Because of the nature of the questions which you have 
posed, our advice has necessarily been somewhat general. 
However, we trust that our analysis has provided you and 
Supervisor Smoley with guidance in analyzing the specific 
factual situations as they arise. 
Should the supervisor 
desire more detailed analysis, with the attendant immunity, 
as to future decisions involving specified entities, please 
do not hesitate to contact our agency. 
Should you have 
further questions regarding this letter, I may be reached at 
916/322-5901.

sections.facts:
(1) 
Sacramento County Supervisor Sandra Smoley (Smoley) 
is an employee of OlDay Travel Agency (OlDay), situated in 
and doing business in Sacramento County. 
(2) 
OIDay is a California, for-profit corporation 
which arranges transportation, lodging and other services 
associated with business and recreational travel. 
With 
respect to domestic travel, OlDay receives a 10% commission 
on the amount of the air fare. 
For foreign travel, the 
commission is 8% of the air fare. 
The commission which 
OlDay receives from lodging and other services is somewhat 
higher than that received from air fare. 
(3) 
Smoley is an employee, not an officer, of OlDay_ 
She does not own stock or have any other proprietary interest 
in OlDay. 
She is paid $500 per month in straight salary 
which compensates Smoley for the services which she performs 
in persuading businesses and other entities to arrange their 
commercial travel through OIDay. 
In addition, she receives 
a commission on these sales. 


Mr. L. B. Elam 
May 10, 1982 
Page Two 
(4) 
Smoley receives a commission upon any contract for 
commercial travel by which a business entity binds itself to 
arrange such travel exclusively through O'Day for a term of 
at least one year. 
These contracts are written, and show 
Smoley as the party who arranged the transaction. 
During 
the first year of such a contract, Smoley receives a commis-
sion of 25% of the commission earned by O'Day on the travel. 
During the second year of the contract, Smoley receives a 
commission of 10% of the commission earned by O'Day. 
Smoley 
receives no commission for travel during any third or later 
year of such a contract. 
Smoley also earns commissions on 
all recreational group tours and recreational cruises arranged 
by her for individuals through O'Day. 
The commission is in 
the amount of 40% of the commission earned by O'Day on such 
individuals. 
In addition, Smoley leads certain of the group 
tours, and the costs are financed through O'Day from charges 
to the individuals participating in the tours.
sections.analysis:
The b~7ic conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act-
(the "Act") are contained in Section 87100, 
which provides: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
Financial interests are defined in Section 87103, the 
pertinent provisions of which are as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on:
